metadata.title: Bear Brown Logo, Every Remotion Move.
metadata.description: A technique-showcase reel animating the Bear Brown full logo mark every way Remotion can — 16:9 landscape edition. One mark (viewBox 0 0 7500 5000, complex single path), 20 techniques, for review: which moves earn their beat?

beats.narration_text: Hey, this is Liam, in for Bear. One logo. Twenty techniques. We're going to run the Bear Brown mark through every motion move Remotion knows, back to back, so you can judge which ones deserve to live.
Spring entrance. The mark arrives with mass — damping set to feel like a dropped object, not a PowerPoint click.
Overshoot spring with squash-and-stretch on landing. Low damping, high stiffness — the mark overshoots its rest position and compresses when it hits.
Draw-on stroke using evolvePath. The pen traces the mark's single complex path — terracotta stroke first, then the ink floods in behind it.
Mask reveal. Linear wipe left to right, then a radial iris opens from the center. Two revealing metaphors, one beat.
Scale zoom, linear versus bezier. The first half scales linearly — mechanical, flat. The second half uses a cubic ease-in-out. The bezier version earns it.
Rotation. Springs in on entrance with a slight pivot, then drifts through one-eighty degrees. Meditative when slow. Restless when fast.
Skew and shear. The mark leans into a skewX transform, holds the tension, then releases back to neutral. Feels italic. Feels rushed.
Opacity through blur. The mark arrives out of focus and sharp at the same time — blur interpolates from eighteen pixels to zero as opacity rises from nothing.
Color interpolation — the color treatment beat. The mark cycles from ink black to terracotta and back. Signal, or decoration? You decide.
Kinetic grid. The mark tiles across the full sixteen-by-nine frame in a staggered ripple — four columns, three rows, each instance springing in with a cascade delay.
Glitch slices. The mark is cut into horizontal strips and each strip randomly offsets on the x-axis for a few frames — aggressive, then instantly gone.
Trail echo. The mark slides across the canvas and five ghost copies trail behind it with decaying opacity — motion that lingers after the fact.
Noise wobble. Three sine waves at different frequencies drive x, y, and rotation independently. Organic or anxious — depends entirely on the amplitude.
Elastic physics. The mark drops in from off-screen, hits the canvas floor, squashes on impact, then bounces back to rest. It hits.
Card flip — rotateY with perspective. The mark flips in from ninety degrees, holds, then spins a full three-sixty. The back face reveals a terracotta accent.
Shadow play. A ghost copy of the mark detaches and drifts behind the original, then snaps back. Detached, then grounded.
Composer summon. We watch the actual prompt get typed into the Claude interface, send fires a terracotta spark, and the mark materializes below. The interface is the origin.
Stroke pulse. The mark switches to outline mode and a stroke-dashoffset heartbeat cycles across the entire path — a rhythm drawn in terracotta.
Scale breathe — a subtle continuous oscillation, just a few percent at about a half-hertz. Barely there. That's the point.
Exit family. Shrink-spin, blur-out, mask-close — three exits, one beat. Most reels only use one. Knowing all three means you can match the exit to what the moment deserves.
Your turn. Take your own logo SVG and run it through spring entrance, draw-on stroke, and card flip. For each technique, build a six-second Remotion composition, then mark it keep or kill.
Liam, in for Bear.
